preFix.title: Mince pie tray slice
preFix.subtitle: Mincemeat filling in a shortcrust traybake
preFix.excerpt: Mince pie tray slice takes the flavours of a traditional mince pie and bakes them as a traybake: a shortcrust base pressed into a rectangular tin, a thick layer of mincemeat, and a crumble or pastry lid. It cuts into neat squares and portions more efficiently than individual pies. Good for feeding a crowd over the Christmas period.
preFix.sourceNotes: Mince pies are one of the oldest British Christmas baked goods, with a history stretching back to the medieval period when they were made with actual minced meat alongside dried fruit and spice. By the eighteenth century the meat had largely disappeared, leaving the fruit and spice mixture recorded in cookery books from Mrs Beeton onward. The tray slice adaptation is a practical modern evolution, appearing in British food magazines and cookery books from the late twentieth century as a more efficient way to produce the same flavour for larger gatherings.

content.text: The pastry for this slice is a simple shortcrust, made more quickly by using a food processor or rubbing in by hand. Press two-thirds of it into the tin as the base and roll the remaining third as a thin top layer. A crumble topping made from the same pastry mixture (rubbed with a little extra butter) can be used instead of a rolled top if you prefer a less formal finish. The mincemeat is used straight from the jar.
Make the pastry and line the tin

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Line a 30 x 20 cm tin with baking paper. Rub 175 g cold diced butter into 350 g plain flour and 50 g icing sugar until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Add 1 beaten egg and bring together into a firm dough, adding a teaspoon of cold water if needed. Divide into two-thirds and one-third. Press the larger portion evenly into the base of the tin, coming up the sides by 1 cm.

Spread 500 g mincemeat evenly over the base. Roll the remaining pastry on a lightly floured surface to fit the top of the tin, or crumble it roughly over the mincemeat for a more textured finish. Bake for 25 to 32 minutes until the top is pale gold and the filling is beginning to bubble at the edges. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes before lifting out using the paper. Cut into squares and dust with icing sugar.

items.problem: The base is soft and not cooked through
items.solution: The base layer was too thick, or the mincemeat was cold and slowed the bake. Press the base to no more than 5 mm. If needed, remove the slice from the tin and return to the oven on the baking paper directly on the shelf for a further 5 minutes.

items.problem: The filling leaked out of the sides
items.solution: The pastry sides were not built up high enough, or there was too much mincemeat. Bring the base pastry up the sides of the tin by at least 1 cm, and keep the mincemeat layer to 5 mm thick.
